worth_it_explanation: DV360 ships with brand safety controls set to permissive defaults. An advertiser that never revisits those defaults may be funding content categories the brand has explicitly said it wants no part of, or running alongside keyword contexts that carry reputational risk. These checks are fast to run inside the DV360 interface and require no external tooling. Most of the work is reading the current settings and comparing them to what the brand has documented as off-limits. The fix when something is wrong is a configuration change, not a campaign rebuild.
intro: Display and Video 360 (DV360) gives advertisers three native layers of brand safety control: sensitive category exclusions, digital content label filters, and keyword and uniform resource locator (URL) exclusion lists. These checks confirm that each layer is actively configured rather than left at the platform's permissive defaults, and that the settings are applied consistently across the line items in an account rather than set on some and missed on others.

audit_checks.title: DV360 native brand safety controls are left at permissive defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Leaving sensitive category exclusions and digital content label filters at their defaults means the account accepts all categories the platform allows by default, including some that most brands explicitly want to avoid. Spend flows to content the brand has not approved, and the exposure accumulates across every impression the line items deliver.

educate.base:
Display and Video 360 (DV360) provides native brand safety controls at the advertiser and line item levels. The two primary mechanisms are sensitive category exclusions and digital content label filters.

Sensitive category exclusions let the advertiser opt out of specific content types, such as tragedy and conflict, sensitive social issues, or profanity and rough language. The full list covers more than a dozen categories. By default, DV360 does not exclude any of them, so a line item with no exclusions will bid on all categories the exchange makes available unless a third-party brand safety vendor narrows the pool further.

Digital content labels classify inventory by audience-appropriateness. The labels, published by the IAB Tech Lab, run from 'G' (suitable for general audiences) through 'DL-MA' (mature audiences). An advertiser can set a maximum label, and DV360 will exclude inventory rated more restrictive than that ceiling. Leaving the label filter unset means the account accepts inventory at all ratings, including ratings the brand may not want.

A few signs point to the controls being at permissive defaults:
  • The advertiser-level brand safety settings show no sensitive categories checked as excluded
  • The digital content label filter is set to 'DL-MA' or shows no maximum label applied
  • Individual line items inherit advertiser defaults without any line-item-level overrides for campaigns that carry higher risk
You may want to compare the current settings against a documented brand safety brief or internal policy before deciding which categories and labels to block.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, the categories most worth reviewing are typically tragedy and conflict, sensitive social issues, and downloads and sharing, where piracy-adjacent inventory sometimes surfaces. You may want to confirm the label ceiling fits the brand's target audience, particularly if the product catalog includes items sold to minors.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, brand proximity matters as much as conversion intent. Content adjacency that raises questions about the advertiser's credibility can reduce form completion rates. You may want to check whether sensitive categories that attract high-anxiety or sensationalist content are excluded. Those contexts can suppress intent even when the ad is served correctly.

learn_more.source_title: About brand safety in Display and Video 360

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser-level brand safety settings and confirm they reflect a deliberate choice, then spot-check a sample of line items for overrides.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: In DV360, open the advertiser and navigate to 'Advertiser settings', then select the 'Brand safety' tab.
Under 'Sensitive categories', review each category listed. Note which are excluded and which are allowed. If none are excluded, record that the control is at its default permissive state.
Under 'Digital content labels', note the maximum content label set. A setting of 'DL-MA' or a blank field indicates no label ceiling has been applied.

Select two or three active line items and open each line item's targeting settings. Confirm whether the line item inherits advertiser-level brand safety settings or has its own overrides. Note any line items where the override is less restrictive than the advertiser default.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the current state of sensitive category exclusions and the digital content label ceiling at the advertiser level, and notes whether any line items carry overrides that weaken those settings. An account with at least one sensitive category excluded and a content label ceiling below 'DL-MA' that aligns with a documented brand policy passes. An account with no category exclusions and no label ceiling applied, or with line items overriding a restrictive advertiser setting to a more permissive one, is a fail recorded with the specific setting and the line items affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the sensitive category exclusions and digital content label ceiling above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Display and Video 360 (DV360) advertiser has native brand safety controls set to permissive defaults, with no sensitive categories excluded and no digital content label ceiling applied. This means the account bids on all content categories the exchange makes available, including categories the brand may have stated as off-limits. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the brand's documented safety policy against the full list of sensitive categories in DV360, applying exclusions that match the policy, and setting a content label ceiling appropriate for the brand's audience. After the changes, confirm that individual line items inherit or extend those settings rather than override them to a more permissive state.

audit_checks.title: Keyword and URL exclusions are absent or not maintained
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without keyword and URL exclusion lists, the account has no way to block adjacency to specific terms or placements that the sensitive category and content label filters do not cover. Categories and labels operate at a broad classification level, so content that passes category filters can still contain specific terms or appear on specific pages the brand wants to avoid.

educate.base:
DV360's sensitive category and content label controls filter at the inventory classification level. They do not allow an advertiser to block adjacency to a specific word, phrase, or domain that falls within an otherwise acceptable category. Keyword exclusions and URL exclusions fill that gap.

Keyword exclusions tell DV360 not to serve on a page whose content contains a specific term. A campaign for a family product can exclude terms like 'violence' or 'gambling' even if the page's category classification does not trigger a category-level block. URL exclusions operate at the domain or page level, preventing the account from serving on specific sites or specific uniform resource locators (URLs) regardless of how the exchange classifies them.

Both exclusion types can be applied at the advertiser level, where they affect all line items, or at the line item level, where they supplement the advertiser list for a specific campaign context. An advertiser with no exclusion lists relies entirely on category filters and label ceilings, which leaves a gap for content that passes those filters but still carries adjacency risk for the brand.

A few signs point to exclusion lists being absent or neglected:
  • The advertiser-level keyword exclusion list is empty or was last updated before any brand safety review
  • No URL exclusion list exists at the advertiser level
  • Line items serving in brand-sensitive placements carry no supplemental exclusions beyond the advertiser defaults
You may want to request the brand's documented exclusion terms from the client or their brand safety team before building the list, rather than relying on intuition alone.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retailers should consider excluding terms related to product recalls, litigation, or competitor brand names when context suggests negative association rather than competitive search intent. URL exclusions are particularly useful for blocking specific user-generated content pages or forum threads that the category filter may pass.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead generation advertisers benefit from URL exclusions on domains that consistently produce low-quality traffic regardless of their content classification. If conversion rate data shows certain domains performing below threshold, those domains are candidates for URL exclusion at the advertiser level so all line items avoid them.

learn_more.source_title: Add keyword exclusions to an advertiser or line item
Exclude specific URLs from your campaign

investigate.intro: Check whether keyword and URL exclusion lists exist at the advertiser level and whether they are applied to the line items serving in brand-sensitive contexts.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: In DV360, open the advertiser and navigate to 'Advertiser settings', then 'Brand safety'.
Find the keyword exclusions section. Note whether a list exists, how many terms it contains, and when it was last modified if the interface shows a timestamp.
Find the URL exclusions section. Note whether a list exists and review a sample of entries to confirm the list reflects current brand policy rather than a default placeholder.

Open two or three active line items that run in brand-sensitive placements. In each line item's targeting, confirm whether the line item carries supplemental keyword or URL exclusions beyond the advertiser-level list. Note any line items with no exclusions at either level.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a keyword exclusion list and a URL exclusion list exist at the advertiser level, whether the lists contain terms reflecting a current brand policy, and whether line items running in brand-sensitive contexts carry supplemental exclusions where appropriate. An account with maintained lists at the advertiser level that reflect the brand's documented policy passes. An account with empty lists, lists that have not been updated since the account launched, or line items in sensitive placements carrying no exclusions at any level is a fail recorded with the specific gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the status of keyword and URL exclusion lists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Display and Video 360 (DV360) account has incomplete or unmaintained keyword and URL exclusion lists, leaving the account reliant on category-level filters alone. Category filters do not block adjacency to specific terms or domains that fall within otherwise acceptable inventory, so brand safety gaps remain for content that passes those filters. {{voice_recommend}} building keyword exclusion lists from the brand's documented off-limits terms, establishing a URL exclusion list covering domains the brand wants to avoid, and applying supplemental exclusions at the line item level for any campaigns running in contexts where brand proximity carries additional risk. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing and updating these lists at least once per quarter as new adjacency risks emerge.

audit_checks.title: Brand safety settings are applied inconsistently across line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When brand safety controls are set at the advertiser level but some line items override them to a more permissive state, or when advertiser defaults are stricter than the brand needs and line items compensate by removing restrictions without documentation, the account operates without a coherent safety posture. Spend from the less-restricted line items exposes the brand even when the overall setup looks protected.

educate.base:
DV360 applies brand safety settings in layers. The advertiser level sets a baseline that all line items inherit. Individual line items can then override those settings, either adding restrictions for a specific campaign context or removing restrictions to reach inventory the advertiser-level settings would otherwise block.

The override capability is intentional. A campaign targeting mature audiences may need to reach inventory that a child-focused campaign would block. The problem arises when overrides are applied without documentation or review, so the account ends up with some line items running under strict controls and others running under permissive ones, and no one has mapped which line items carry which posture.

A few signs point to inconsistent application:
  • Line items in the same insertion order (IO) carry different sensitive category exclusions with no documented reason
  • Some line items inherit advertiser-level brand safety settings and others have overrides, but the overrides are not documented in the IO or line item naming convention
  • A line item serving the same audience and creative as a sibling line item carries a more permissive content label ceiling with no rationale recorded
You may want to build a simple inventory of line item brand safety postures before making changes, rather than assuming the advertiser defaults are applied uniformly.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running multiple simultaneous campaigns, prospecting line items and retargeting line items may legitimately carry different brand safety settings if the audiences they reach differ in context. You may want to confirm the difference is intentional and document it rather than treating all inconsistency as a problem to fix.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser with campaigns across multiple products or verticals, brand safety settings that are appropriate for one product line may not be appropriate for another. You may want to confirm that differences between line items reflect those product-level decisions rather than configuration drift.

investigate.intro: Survey the brand safety settings across a representative sample of active line items and map where settings diverge from the advertiser defaults.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: In DV360, open the advertiser and note the advertiser-level brand safety settings as the baseline: which sensitive categories are excluded and what content label ceiling is set.
Navigate to 'Line items' and filter for active line items. Select five to ten line items that span different campaigns or insertion orders.

For each selected line item, open its targeting settings and find the brand safety section. Note whether the line item inherits advertiser defaults or carries its own overrides. Record any overrides that are more permissive than the advertiser baseline.
Note whether any of the more permissive overrides are documented in the line item name, notes field, or the parent insertion order's description.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the advertiser-level brand safety baseline and the settings for each sampled line item, noting where line items diverge. Line items with overrides that are stricter than the advertiser baseline pass without question. Line items with overrides that are more permissive than the advertiser baseline pass only when the deviation is documented and maps to a deliberate campaign decision. Line items with undocumented permissive overrides are a fail recorded with the line item name and the specific setting that diverges.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the brand safety settings for the sampled line items above to compare against the advertiser baseline.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that brand safety settings are applied inconsistently across {{site_name}}'s Display and Video 360 (DV360) line items. Some line items inherit the advertiser-level controls while others carry undocumented overrides that allow content the advertiser baseline would block. This means spend from the less-restricted line items exposes the brand even when the overall account setup appears protected. {{voice_recommend}} auditing all active line items against the advertiser-level baseline, removing permissive overrides that lack a documented rationale, and establishing a naming or notes convention that records when a line item intentionally deviates from the advertiser brand safety defaults.
